beats.narration: Right now, sitting perfectly still, you feel like nothing around you is moving. That stillness is the greatest illusion of your life.
beats.understand: the VISUAL contradicts the words: you feel still, but the screen proves you are hurtling through space

beats.narration: We think we know what fast is. A sprinter, twenty-seven. A jet, five hundred sixty. A rifle bullet, two thousand one hundred miles an hour.
beats.understand: anchor everyday 'fast' so the reveal later dwarfs it

beats.narration: But the ground beneath your feet is not still. The Earth spins at over a thousand miles an hour — about half the speed of that bullet, and you never feel it.
beats.understand: the ground you stand on is itself moving faster than a bullet

beats.narration: Now pull back, and watch what our whole planet is really doing.
beats.understand: zoom out from the spin to the orbit

beats.narration: Earth is falling around the Sun at sixty-six thousand miles an hour. Eighteen miles every single second.
And the Sun itself is not still. It drags us around the galaxy at half a million miles an hour — a tiny world, riding a star, around a galaxy that is itself racing through the universe.
beats.understand: the headline number — our galactic speed — rides the galaxy as it pulls back continuously into the full spiral (one climax, not two identical zooms). The galaxy cannot be filmed from inside it: designed 2D, tier C.

beats.narration: You have never been still. Not for one single second of your life.
beats.understand: THE thesis lands over the calm Earth from the opening, reinterpreted (intentional callback); the film ENDS here
